teaching_arc.entry_point: Begin by connecting back to Session 1: students prepared Ubuntu because today we turn the laptop into a reliable ROS 2 lab machine.
teaching_arc.progression: Move from terminal habits and Linux paths into ROS 2 Humble installation, environment sourcing, one course workspace, underlay and overlay, verification, and previews of rqt, RViz2, and Gazebo.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a working setup and a simple rule for future labs: source the right environment, know where you are, build cleanly, source the overlay, and verify with ROS tools.

audience_level: undergraduate robotics lab students setting up Ubuntu 22.04 and ROS 2 Humble for the first time, with mixed terminal experience
topic_goal: Turn the installation from Session 1 into a reliable ROS 2 working environment and teach the terminal habits students will need for every manipulator lab.
topic_takeaways: Students should be able to navigate the terminal without feeling lost.
Students should understand why sourcing ROS and sourcing the workspace overlay changes what commands and packages are visible.
Students should have one course workspace and know the build-source-run rhythm.
Students should be ready for Session 3, where these setup habits become ROS graph and debugging habits.
style_notes: Speak like a professor guiding students through setup, not like a command manual.
For command-heavy slides, explain the purpose and verification signal rather than reciting every command.
Repeat the idea that students should debug in order: environment first, workspace second, package third.
Close by making Session 3 feel natural: now that the machine is ready, we can understand what ROS is running.
transition_style: Connect each setup habit to the next practical consequence, then close each section with what students should verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Open by saying that today is the bridge between installation and the manipulator workflow. The goal is not theory first; the goal is a reliable machine and reliable habits.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students understand ubuntu terminal ros2 setup in a way they can explain, apply, and build on later.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Manipulators In Simulation so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Session 2 - Ubuntu Terminal + ROS 2 Setup as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Session 2 - Ubuntu Terminal + ROS 2 Setup connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Session 2 - Ubuntu Terminal + ROS 2 Setup sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Keep this short. Students only need the course direction: manipulator systems, simulation-first workflow, and ROS tools that build toward MoveIt later.
must_cover: This course uses robot manipulators.
We work in simulation first.
The stack is Ubuntu 22.04 -> ROS 2 Humble -> RViz2 / rqt -> Gazebo -> MoveIt.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Today's Goal so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
prop_suggestions: MoveIt Documentation - MoveIt Quickstart in RViz

slides.story_hint: Keep Manipulators In Simulation connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Manipulators In Simulation clearly before the formal details continue.

slides.narration_seed: This is the contract for the session. It gives students the practical finish line before the next manipulator topics.
must_cover: Use basic Ubuntu terminal commands without fear.
Install and source ROS 2 Humble correctly.
Create one clean course workspace.

slides.narration_seed: Robotics students do not need to become Linux administrators today. They do need to become comfortable enough that the terminal stops being a blocker.
must_cover: The terminal is the main robotics control surface.
You install packages from it.
You build workspaces from it.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Linux Paths You Must Know so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Make sure students can say what each path means. This one slide prevents many later workspace mistakes.
must_cover: / = root of the whole filesystem
/home/ = your personal files
~ = shortcut for your home folder
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Terminal Habits That Save Time so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Have students practice Tab completion and history in class. These habits reduce typing errors immediately.
must_cover: Tab completes commands, folders, and many package names.
Up and Down move through command history.
Ctrl+C stops a running command or ROS node.
Ctrl+L clears the terminal screen.
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep Terminal Habits That Save Time efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on Terminal Habits That Save Time and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: The biggest beginner terminal mistake is running a command from the wrong folder. Use pwd often.
must_cover: pwd -> print the current folder
ls -> list files and folders
ls -la -> list hidden files too
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Navigation Commands to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Navigation Commands is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.story_hint: Keep Navigation Commands connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Navigation Commands accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Say explicitly that rm does not use a friendly recycle bin in the way students may expect. They should check paths first.
must_cover: mkdir lab1 -> create a folder
touch notes.txt -> create an empty file
cp notes.txt backup.txt -> copy a file
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Files And Folders to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Files And Folders is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Files And Folders accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Nano is enough for today. Mention Ctrl+O to save and Ctrl+X to exit if you demo it.
must_cover: cat file.txt -> print the whole file
less file.txt -> scroll through a file; press q to quit
head file.txt -> show the first lines
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Viewing And Editing Text Files to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Viewing And Editing Text Files is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Keep the language precise. update does not update the installed software; it updates package information.
must_cover: update refreshes the package list.
upgrade updates installed packages.
install downloads a prebuilt package and puts it in system locations.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use APT Package Management to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once APT Package Management is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Students will see newer tutorials online. Tell them newer is not automatically correct for a shared course stack.
must_cover: Use Ubuntu 22.04 LTS for this course.
Use ROS 2 Humble for this course.
Do not switch to Ubuntu 24.04 / Jazzy for this class.

slides.narration_seed: This follows the ROS Humble install guidance. A fresh Ubuntu 22.04 system should be upgraded before ROS install work.
must_cover: Restart if Ubuntu asks you to restart.
A clean base system avoids package conflicts later.

slides.narration_seed: This is the current-terminal version. The next slides add the same base source line to .bashrc so new terminals behave correctly.
must_cover: which ros2 should point to a ROS executable.
printenv ROS_DISTRO should print humble.
If ros2 is missing, your shell environment is not ready.

slides.narration_seed: Explain that .bashrc is powerful but should stay boring. We add only predictable lines.
must_cover: Every new interactive terminal reads ~/.bashrc.
We use it to source ROS 2 Humble automatically.
We also add small aliases to reduce repeated typing.

must_cover: eb edits .bashrc.
sb reloads .bashrc.
Keep this block minimal and readable.

slides.narration_seed: One clean workspace prevents most early path confusion. Do not let students scatter packages across home.
must_cover: colcon build reads packages in src/ and creates build/, install/, and log/.
Source the overlay so your terminal can find what you just built.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What colcon build Creates so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Create One Course Workspace first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.

slides.narration_seed: Students should understand that build, install, and log are generated by colcon. They should not place source code directly in those folders.
must_cover: src/ -> source code for packages you write or clone
build/ -> temporary compile and generation files
install/ -> finished package output for this workspace
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into .bashrc Part 2: Workspace Aliases so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for What colcon build Creates first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.

slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: The separation is intentional. Students should learn that building and sourcing are different steps.
must_cover: ws jumps to the workspace root.
src jumps to the package folder.
cb builds; si sources. Keep these separate at first.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use .bashrc Part 2: Workspace Aliases to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once .bashrc Part 2: Workspace Aliases is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: This matches the documented colcon_cd setup and the user's terminal block. Keep it as the familiar course helper block.
must_cover: Add the helper block on the right to .bashrc.
colcon_cd jumps directly to a package folder.
Tab completion helps complete colcon commands and options.

slides.narration_seed: Use one sentence: the underlay is the base ROS installation, and the overlay is what you just built in your workspace.
must_cover: /opt/ros/humble is the underlay.
~/ros2_ws/install is your overlay.
Source the base first.

slides.narration_seed: The goal is diagnostic reflexes. When a system does not work, first ask what nodes and topics actually exist.
must_cover: ros2 pkg list -> list installed packages
ros2 node list -> list running nodes
ros2 topic list -> list active topics

slides.narration_seed: Connect this to future manipulator systems. When many nodes run together, the graph is the fastest first inspection tool.
must_cover: rqt_graph draws nodes and topics visually.
Use it to see who publishes and who subscribes.
Open it early instead of guessing.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into RViz2 Preview so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: This distinction matters. Gazebo simulates physics and worlds; RViz visualizes ROS data.
must_cover: RViz2 is visualization, not simulation.
Use it to inspect robot state, frames, markers, and trajectories.
Later, we will use RViz2 with robot arms and MoveIt.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Gazebo For Later Simulation so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for RViz2 Preview first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture RViz2 Preview clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Show the robot model, TF, and RViz displays as a simple pipeline so students can connect each layer to the visual outcome.

slides.narration_seed: Keep this as a preview only. The point today is not Gazebo skills; it is keeping the version path consistent.
must_cover: Gazebo will be our simulation environment later.
For Humble, use the tested course path:
Humble's default Gazebo pairing through this path is Fortress.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What Not To Do Today so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: This slide is protective. MoveIt is important, but the base stack must be stable first.
must_cover: Do not install random newer tutorial stacks.
Do not switch to Ubuntu 24.04 / Jazzy for this course.
Do not force a full MoveIt source build today.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Gazebo For Later Simulation into What Not To Do Today so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Common Setup Errors so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use What Not To Do Today to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep What Not To Do Today connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how What Not To Do Today sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: The debugging order is: check source, check workspace, check package, then investigate deeper.
must_cover: ros2: command not found -> ROS base was not sourced.
package not found -> package missing, wrong terminal, or overlay not sourced.
Built successfully but old behavior remains -> source the overlay again.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Common Setup Errors to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Common Setup Errors is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.story_hint: Keep Common Setup Errors connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Common Setup Errors accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Close by treating this as a readiness check for Session 3. Students should leave knowing the machine is ready and that next time we use these tools to inspect the ROS graph.
must_cover: Ubuntu is updated.
ROS 2 Humble is installed.
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash works.
The course workspace builds, sources, and can run the talker/listener test.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use End Checklist to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by saying that Session 3 will use this exact setup to understand packages, nodes, topics, rqt_graph, and debugging.
slides.explanation_style: Use End Checklist as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep End Checklist connected to the next lab: a clean setup lets the class spend time understanding ROS instead of fixing missing environment variables.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave with a reliable setup checklist and a clear expectation for Session 3.
